headline: How Uber uses EBS volume types to power real-time maps and trip data
why: Uber's map matching engine processes millions of GPS pings per second. The difference between gp2, io2, and st1 EBS volumes translates directly into whether a driver gets matched to a rider in 1 second or 10 seconds.

topics.usage: Uber's PostgreSQL databases for active trips run on io2 volumes with 64,000 IOPS. Every GPS ping from every driver triggers a DB write. Low latency here = faster matching = happier riders.
Driver profile data, trip history, and user accounts use gp3 — good performance at reasonable cost. These don't need 64k IOPS, but they need consistent response times.
Uber stores months of raw GPS telemetry logs on st1 volumes — cheap HDD storage optimised for sequential reads. Used by the data team for route optimisation ML training, not real-time queries.

Uber's real-time geospatial index (the grid that snaps GPS coordinates to road segments) lives in Instance Store — NVMe SSDs directly on the host. Rebuilt on restart from S3. Fastest possible read, zero persistence needed.

diagram:
graph LR
    GPS([📍 Driver GPS Ping]) --> Match[Map Matching Engine\nInstance Store NVMe\nFastest reads]
    Match --> DB[Active Trips DB\nio2 — 64k IOPS\nLow latency writes]
    DB --> Profile[Driver Profiles\ngp3 — balanced\ncost + performance]
    DB --> Archive[Raw GPS Logs\nst1 HDD\nCheap sequential]
    Archive --> ML[ML Training\nRoute Optimisation]
    style GPS fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8
    style Match fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style DB fill:#450a0a,color:#fca5a5
    style Profile fill:#1e3a5f,color:#dbeafe
    style Archive fill:#1a3320,color:#86efac
    style ML fill:#1a3320,color:#86efac
takeaway: Uber uses 4 different storage types on the same workload — not because it's complex, but because each layer has different latency and cost requirements. Picking the right EBS type per workload saves millions and keeps the app fast.
